coredata.dc:description:
To improve the water environment quality, the development of an effective photocatalyst for pollutant removal was considered a promising strategy. The aim of the development of a novel photocatalyst PNC is pursued by modifying copper-phenylacetylide (PhC2Cu) with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs). Leading to a remarkable improvement in its light absorption capability, electron transfer efficiency and photoelectrochemical properties. Importantly, PNC possesses the characteristic of straightforward synthesis and demonstrates remarkable performance in the photodegradation of 99.87% sulfamethoxazole (SMX) within just 15 min, with a 3.95-fold increase in the photocatalytic rate. Analysis of the active substances revealed that 1O2,
O 2
· ‐
, and h+ are the generated active species by PNC. Active sites and degradation pathways of SMX were explored through density functional theory (DFT) calculations and intermediate analysis. Key evidence regarding the direction of electron transfer within the system was obtained through in-situ irradiated X-ray (ISI-XPS) techniques. This study deepened our understanding of the electron transfer characteristics of phenylacetylene copper and provided new insights for the modification of photocatalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
To improve the water environment quality, the development of an effective photocatalyst for pollutant removal was considered a promising strategy. The aim of the development of a novel photocatalyst PNC is pursued by modifying copper-phenylacetylide (PhC2Cu) with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs). Leading to a remarkable improvement in its light absorption capability, electron transfer efficiency and photoelectrochemical properties. Importantly, PNC possesses the characteristic of straightforward synthesis and demonstrates remarkable performance in the photodegradation of 99.87% sulfamethoxazole (SMX) within just 15 min, with a 3.95-fold increase in the photocatalytic rate. Analysis of the active substances revealed that 1O2,
O 2
· ‐
, and h+ are the generated active species by PNC. Active sites and degradation pathways of SMX were explored through density functional theory (DFT) calculations and intermediate analysis. Key evidence regarding the direction of electron transfer within the system was obtained through in-situ irradiated X-ray (ISI-XPS) techniques. This study deepened our understanding of the electron transfer characteristics of phenylacetylene copper and provided new insights for the modification of photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfamethoxazole (SMX) is one of the most widely used antibiotics at present, utilized for treating diseases such as urinary tract infections, gastrointestinal infections, and otitis media (Jahdi et al., 2020). However, due to the fact that this drug is not removed by conventional wastewater treatment methods, it was detected in almost all representative environmental compartments, with drug residues being discovered (Liang et al., 2021; Thiebault, 2020). It was reported that the concentration of SMX in aquaculture, pharmaceutical, and municipal wastewater could be as high as mg·L−1 (Lin and Tsai, 2009; Xu et al., 2011). These pollutants are characterized by low biodegradability, high toxicity, and persistence, posing a serious threat to aquatic ecosystems and humans (Zhang et al., 2021, 2019). Therefore, there was an urgent need to develop an effective treatment technology to remove organic pollutants from the environment. Common SMX treatment technologies, including physical adsorption (Sarker et al., 2023), biodegradation (Zhao et al., 2023), advanced oxidation (Tian et al., 2019), and photocatalysis (Liu et al., 2024), all showed good treatment effects. Among them, photocatalysis was considered an ideal technological approach for SMX treatment due to its advantages of being less polluting, energy-efficient, and environmentally friendly. Hence, the development of an efficient treatment photocatalyst to enhance its removal rate in SMX treatment was deemed of great significance.

Copper-phenylacetylide (PhC2Cu) has been demonstrated to be a p-type semiconductor with broad visible light responsiveness, and its long-range ordered structure is advantageous for interfacial electron transfer, making it a typical coordination polymer (Zhang et al., 2023). It is noteworthy that the characteristic of the relatively negative conduction band potential (−2.01 V vs. NHE) of PhC2Cu endowed it with excellent reduction performance (Wang et al., 2021). However, due to PhC2Cu's weak oxidation ability, superoxide generation rate, and photocarrier transfer capability, achieving high-efficiency photocatalytic activity was challenging. Hence, there was an urgent need to establish an efficient photocatalyst and optimize its performance.

Modifying materials with co-catalysts (e.g., carbon materials: graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanoparticles) is deemed an effective approach to enhancing the photocatalytic activity of semiconductors (Zhao et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2020). The primary functions include inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated carriers and serving as active sites for photocatalytic reactions (Xia et al., 2021). Therefore, in this study, a hybrid of PhC2Cu nanowires and carbon nanoparticles was designed to overcome the aforementioned drawbacks. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are commonly used nanostructures with sizes typically ranging from 2 to 10 nm (Zuo et al., 2016). They have garnered significant attention due to their simple structure, high controllability, good biocompatibility, and excellent water solubility. Research has demonstrated that introducing CDs into photocatalysis can enhance visible light absorption and improve electron transfer efficiency (Chauhan et al., 2021; Jin et al., 2016; Keçili et al., 2023). Importantly, the introduction of N atoms into CDs, formed nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs), can stimulate the activity of the adjacent positively charged Sp2-C, providing more reactive sites and generating more active species (Zhao et al., 2023). Additionally, N-CDs, function as electron donors, injecting electrons into PhC2Cu, thereby ameliorating its photoinduced charge recombination. Consequently, this strategy can substantially enhance the utilization of PhC2Cu's photogenerated electrons on conduction band (CB), resulting in higher photocatalyst performance.

The study proposed to prepare PNC using a simple and easy-to-operate method, evaluating the photocatalytic activity of PNC through the degradation of SMX under the illumination of a blue LED lamp. Simultaneously, a series of characterizations were conducted on PNC to demonstrate its efficient photocatalytic efficiency and the feasibility of this photocatalytic system in the environment. Additionally, electron paramagnetic resonance (ESR), in-situ irradiated X-ray (ISI-XPS), and density functional theory (DFT) calculations were employed to further elucidate the electron transfer mechanism within the system and propose a reasonable reaction mechanism. An analysis of the transformation products of SMX was conducted, and the degradation pathways were explored. Lastly, the practicality of the photocatalytic system in a natural aquatic environment was assessed by analyzing the impacts of water quality parameters and the natural water matrix.

0.200 g of Cu(OAc)2·H2O was added into a beaker, followed by adding 50 mL of methanol (CH3OH), 0.176 g of L-histidine (C6H8O6) and 0.12 mL of phenylacetylene (C8H6); Stir the mixture for 1.5 h at room temperature. Transfer the solution to a 100 mL centrifuge tube and centrifuge for 10 min at 10,000 r·min−1. Rinse the resulting precipitate twice with ultrapure water and once with ethanol (C2H5OH). The final PhC2Cu (Fig. S1) powder was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 8 h, then ground and collected it (Zhao et al., 2022).

N-CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method (Wang et al., 2017). 3.00 g of citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O) and 1.50 g of urea (H2NCONH2) were dissolved in 15 mL of ultrapure water by grinding. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave with polytetrafluoroethylene and heat-treated at 180 °C for 5 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove aggregated particles, and the solution was adjusted to a neutral pH with a hydrochloric acid solution to obtain the N-CDs solution. After 10 h of vacuum drying at 60 °C, the final N-CDs powder was obtained.

0.100 g of PhC2Cu was introduced into a 25 mL beaker containing 20 mL of C2H5OH. After thorough ultrasonic stirring at room temperature, specific amounts of N-CDs (0.5%, 1%, 2%, and 4%) were incorporated, and stirring was continued for several hours. The resulting PNC powder was subsequently vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 8 h and then collected.

In the photocatalysis experiments, a 9 W LED blue lamp (455 nm) served as a visible light source. The procedure involved adding 0.020 g of photocatalyst and 50 mL of 5 mg L−1 SMX solution into a 50 mL beaker, followed by complete dispersed in a sonicator. Subsequently, the beaker was placed on a stirrer, and the dark adsorption and photocatalytic reaction experiments were conducted under stirring, with the blue lamp irradiation. Samples were collected at specific time intervals (0, 3, 6, 9, 12, and 15 min), filtered through a 0.45 μm filter, and tested for the residual concentration of SMX in the samples by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, 20 A, Shimadzu, Japan) (Chen et al., 2023).

After utilizing a one-step technique to synthesize PhC2Cu (Fig. S2a) and 0.5 PN C. Electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were utilized to observe the morphology and structure of samples. As portrayed in Fig. 1 a, a typical nanowire structure with a diameter of about 20–30 nm was exhibited by PhC2Cu. After the modification by N-CDs, the morphology and structure of PhC2Cu did not experience significant changes (Fig. 1b). In Fig. 1c, N-CDs were distributed on the surface of the nanowires of PhC2Cu and a tighter bond with PhC2Cu was formed. To further confirm the existence of N-CDs, an analysis was conducted using a high-magnification HETEM image (Fig. 1d), which revealed N-CDs lattice streaks with a plane distance of 0.22 nm on PhC2Cu. And it exhibited a wide peak at 2θ = 24.45°(Fig. S2b), corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon (Wang et al., 2017). These phenomena demonstrated the successful introduction of N-CDs into PhC2Cu.

The traits on the exterior of the samples were explored using nitrogen adsorption-desorption method. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, both PhC2Cu and 0.5 PN C exhibited type IV adsorption-desorption isotherms with H3 hysteresis lines (Fig. S3). The specific surface area (SBET) of 0.5 PN C was measured at 89.146 m2 g−1 surpassed that of PhC2Cu (65.948 m2 g−1), attributed to the introduction of the porous structure and the superior specific surface area of N-CDs. This enhancement significantly contributes to the surface reaction, introducing more active sites and improving the adsorption capacity between catalysts and the target pollutants. Ultimately, this facilitated higher efficient activation of dissolved oxygen molecules in water, generating more active species and thereby boosting photocatalytic efficiency (Zhang et al., 2024).

To delve deeper into the chemical characteristics of the created samples, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) examinations were conducted on PhC2Cu, 0.5 PN C, 1 PN C, 2 PN C, and 4 PN C. In Fig. 2b, characteristic C–CC and C C–Cu belonging to PhC2Cu were observed at 518 cm−1 and 1930 cm−1 peaks (Wei et al., 2018). The characteristic absorption peaks located at 684 cm−1 and 748 cm−1 were attributed to C–C and C–H (Zhang et al., 2023). Furthermore, the peaks located at 1440 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 were assigned to CO and CN, respectively. Notably, the intensities of the peak signals showed a gradual enhancement as the proportion of N-CDs increased. This served as further evidence of the successful attachment of N-CDs to PhC2Cu (Chen et al., 2023).

To gain deeper insights into the physicochemical interactions of the prepared samples, X-ray photoelectron spectrometers (XPS) analysis was performed. As portrayed in Fig. 2c, in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum, the peak at 284.4 eV corresponds to CC, the peak at 285.2 eV corresponds to C C, and the peak at 289.4 eV corresponds to CC–O (Chen et al., 2023). Additionally, the two peaks in the Cu 2p spectrum in Fig. 2d correspond to Cu 2p 3/2 and Cu 2p 1/2 with binding energies of 932.8 eV and 952.7 eV, respectively (Zhang et al., 2023). It is noteworthy that the peaks in the Cu 2p spectra shifted to higher binding energy positions in the 0.5 PN C. This implied that there was an interaction between PhC2Cu and N-CDs driven by the built-in electric field (IEF), which served as an efficient electron transfer (Li et al., 2022).

As illustrated in Fig. 2e, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs in aqueous solution revealed two characteristic peaks at 246 nm and 350 nm, possibly arising from π-π* transitions in the aromatic sp 2 conjugative domain and n-π* transitions in the CO band (Zhang et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2013). In Fig. 2f, the upconversion radiation of N-CDs was observed in the range of 350 nm–700 nm under light excitation ranging from 600–800 nm. These findings suggested that the prepared N-CDs could have improved photocatalytic efficiency by enhancing electron transfer capacity and boosting light absorption.

UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy was employed to explore the light absorption characteristics of PhC2Cu and 0.5 PN C. The outcome displayed in Fig. S4 illustrated that PhC2Cu exhibited a conventional absorption edge band located around 495 nm, in contrast to 0.5 PN C's shifted absorption edge band at roughly 506 nm, which indicated a broader utilization of the light energy, aligning well with the application characteristics of carbon dots on modified materials. Tauc plots (Fig. S5, Eq.S1) were analyzed to examine the energy band structure of PhC2Cu and 0.5 PN C, with calculated band gap widths of 2.55 eV and 2.52 eV, respectively.

From cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis (Fig. S6), oxidation and reduction potentials of −0.88 V and 1.48 V, respectively, for PhC2Cu, determining a valence band (EVB) of 0.54 V vs. NHE. For 0.5 PN C, oxidation and reduction potentials were −0.82 V and 1.44 V, respectively, resulting in an EVB of 0.55 V vs. NHE. Eventually, the conduction band (ECB) of PhC2Cu and 0.5 PN C was determined to be - 2.01 V and - 1.97 V vs. NHE, respectively (Eqs.S2∼S3). Additionally, CV provided evidence that N-CDs act as electron donors, transferring electrons to PhC2Cu. This results in 0.5 PN C exhibited a higher redox potential compared to PhC2Cu (Lin et al., 2023).

Efficient separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) are crucial for semiconductor photocatalytic activity. Photoluminescence (PL) analysis aids in assessing this efficiency. Notably, the PL fluorescence intensity of 0.5 PN C was lower than that of PhC2Cu (Fig. 3 a), which suggested that the recombination of photogenerated electrons may have been inhibited by the presence of N-CDs. Additionally, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra were performed to detect the average carrier lifetime (τave) of PhC2Cu and 0.5 PN C (Fig. 3b and Table S2). The results illustrated the average fluorescence lifetimes of 4.84 ns and 3.24 ns for PhC2Cu and 0.5 PN C, respectively. In comparison to PhC2Cu, the τave of 0.5 PN C was considerably attenuated, suggesting that in 0.5 PN C the recombination of photogenerated e− and h+ was effectively suppressed (Li et al., 2021).

In addition, the exciton dynamics within the catalyst were further investigated by testing the instantaneous photocurrent density response curve (i-t) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the photocatalyst. Fig. 3c and d displays the instantaneous photocurrent response curves and electrochemical impedance spectra of PhC2Cu and 0.5 PN C, respectively. The resistance value of 0.5 PN C is 5.17 KΩ, which is significantly lower than that of PhC2Cu (129.81 KΩ) (Sapna et al., 2018). The 0.5 PN C exhibited enhanced photocurrent intensity and a smaller radius of curvature compared to PhC2Cu. These results demonstrated that a stronger photoresponsivity and lower internal resistance were observed in 0.5 PN C, which were attributed to the electron conduction properties of N-CDs, which further enhanced exciton separation capability and photocatalytic performance (Xie et al., 2018).

The pathway of photogenerated electron transfer between N-CDs and PhC2Cu under light conditions was further verified using ISI-XPS. As depicted in Fig. 4 f, after exposuring to light, a red shift was observed in the Cu 2p spectrum of 0.5 PN C, suggesting that electrons were accepted by the Cu atoms. In comparison to pure PhC2Cu, when illuminated, it primarily shows a deficit of electrons on its Cu atoms (Zhang et al., 2023). According to the electrostatic shielding effect, the binding energy is weaker when there are more electrons in the outer layers, indicating that the photogenerated electrons excited by N-CDs under light conditions were transferred to PhC2Cu (Zhang et al., 2020). Therefore, solid evidence of the photogenerated electron transfer pathway within the system was provided by exploring the transfer of Cu 2p binding energy in 0.5 PN C. These results confirmed the successful construction of PCN through the introduction of N-CDs, showcased notable upconversion fluorescence properties. This effectively facilitated the rapid separation of PhC2Cu excitons, aligned with our initial expectations. Next, we will delve into the changes in photocatalytic performance from a broader perspective.

The surface reaction of the catalyst is crucial for photocatalytic efficiency. Prior to the photocatalysis experiments, the adsorption capability of SMX by the photocatalysts was investigated (Fig. S8a). Notably, N-CDs exhibited minimal adsorption capability, whereas PhC2Cu adsorbed 27.74% and 0.5 PN C adsorbed 38.82%. The enhanced adsorption capacity was in line with the findings of the BET analysis in the preamble, indicating that the specific surface area of the material could be increased by this modification strategy, which improved the adsorption capacity of the material, and eventually contributed to the enhancement of its photocatalyst performance.

In the photocatalytic experiment, the degradation rate of SMX by PhC2Cu was only 79.78% (Fig. 4a). And the photocatalytic effect of SMX degradation by N-CDs was almost negligible. In contrast, SMX was degraded up to 99.87% in 15 min by the optimal 0.5 PN C, with a calculated reaction rate of 0.4395 min−1 (Fig. S8b) according to the Pseudo-first-order dynamics formula (Eqs.S4∼S5), which was 3.95 times higher than that of PhC2Cu, in line with the previous electrochemical and photoluminescence test results, indicating that the enhanced photocatalytic performance was a result of the efficient separation of carriers. Besides, the photodegradation rate of SMX decreased gradually with an increase in N-CDs content, which might be attributed to the light shielding effect of N-CDs, leading to a decrease in available photons in the PNC. Table S3 illustrated the degradation of SMX by other photocatalysts as reported, showing that 0.5 PN C exhibited superior degradation efficiency when exposed to visible light. Meanwhile, according to the estimation of Eqs.S6, PNC system possessed excellent economic benefits and feasibility in practical application (Table S4) (Ding and Hu, 2020; Lin et al., 2024).

In the photocatalytic process, there were many active substances directly or indirectly involved in the reaction, which in turn degrades SMX. To study the free radicals involved in the 0.5 PN C photocatalytic system, ESR tests were carried out in this paper on PhC2Cu and 0.5 PN C systems. In these tests, DMPO was conducted as a spin trapping agent for ·OH and
O 2
· ‐
while TEMP was employed as a spin trapping agent for 1O2. The results are shown in Fig. 4b, c, and 4d. The ESR signal intensity was not evident under dark conditions. The signal intensities of DMPO-·OH、DMPO-
O 2
· ‐
, and TEMP-1O2 exhibited an enhancement in 0.5 PN C compared to PhC2Cu. With the introduction of N-CDs, PhC2Cu acquired more active reaction sites and thus more active species were produced in the 0.5 PN C system.

To verify the primary active substances acting on the materials, free radical quenching experiments were performed in this paper. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA) was used for quenching 1, p-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl radical (TEMPO) was employed for trapping
O 2
· ‐
, sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) was utilized for quenching h+, potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) was applied for quenching e−, and furfuryl alcohol (FFA) was used for quenching 1O2 during the experiment (Wang et al., 2024). As illustrated in Fig. 4e, the active substances that served as the primary contributors to the degradation of SMX were 1O2,
O 2
· ‐
, and h+ contributed 76.91%, 53.51% and 44.40% respectively (Eqs.S7∼S11). Moreover, for further verification of the production through the activation of O2 in water, photocatalytic experiments were carried out under O2 and N2 aeration conditions to evaluate the role of oxygen, respectively. The results indicated that the photocatalytic performance of 0.5 PN C was enhanced under an O2 atmosphere, while the opposite effect was observed under an N2 atmosphere, highlighting the crucial role of O2 in the transfer of active substances.

To further elucidate the generation of
O 2
· ‐
and 1O2 by PhC2Cu and 0.5 PN C under visible light irradiation, nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) and furfuryl alcohol (FFA) were used as molecular probes, respectively, to quantify the amounts of
O 2
· ‐
and 1O2 by spectroscopy (Fang et al., 2023). The
O 2
· ‐
was captured on the surface of the photocatalysts by NBT molecules, generating formaldehyde derivatives, and the concentration of the generated
O 2
· ‐
was determined by the degradation of NBT. As depicted in Fig. S9a, after 60 min of light exposure, the steady-state concentrations generated by PhC2Cu and 0.5 PN C were reached at 1.66 × 10−7 M and 2.26 × 10−7 M, respectively. The steady-state concentration of 1O2 was determined by reacting FFA molecules with 1O2, as outlined in the experimental procedure in the SI. As illustrated in Fig. S9b, the steady-state concentrations of 1O2 generated by PhC2Cu and 0.5 PN C were calculated at 1.38 × 10−11 M and 2.29 × 10−11 M, respectively. The outcomes indicated that the emergence of N-CDs in the 0.5 PN C remarkably amplified the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS).

The degradation of SMX was effectively enhanced by 0.5 PN C with electronic transfer and photovoltaic properties, a photocatalytic mechanism for the 0.5 PN C system was proposed (Fig. 5 ). Under visible light irradiation, photogenerated-hole pairs were generated on 0.5 PN C. Since the CB position of 0.5 PN C (−1.97 V vs. NHE) is more negative than the standard reduction potential of O2/
O 2
· ‐
(−0.33 V vs. NHE), the photogenerated e− in the CB readily reacts with molecular oxygen. However, the valence band (VB) position of 0.5 PN C (0.55 V vs. NHE) is much lower than the standard oxidation potential of OH−/·OH (1.99 V vs. NHE), and the photogenerated h+ cannot oxidize OH− and H2O to generate ·OH. Fortunately, the enhancement of the adsorption capacity of O2 by the porous structure of the photocatalyst facilitated the O2 reduction reaction and the rapid separation of photogenerated carriers, leading to the increased generation of ·OH. The transformation relationship of free radicals in the system and the primary degradation process of SMX in this system are illustrated in Eqs. (1)–(8) (M. Chen et al., 2019; P. Chen et al., 2019):

The photocatalytic degradation mechanism between organic molecules and SMX was investigated by performing DFT calculations on the structure of SMX to identify the active site of organic molecules. The capacity to lose or acquire electrons is commonly evaluated through the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) (Li et al., 2020). The LUMO of SMX is primarily resides on the benzene ring and sulfonamide groups, making them susceptible to nucleophilic group attacks (Fig. 6 a) (Su et al., 2022). Additionally, the highest HOMO indicates that electrons are easily prone to escape. Additionally, the highest HOMO facilitates the easy escape of electrons (Peng et al., 2021). In Fig. 6b, the HOMO orbitals of SMX are predominantly located on one side of the benzene ring, in the vicinity of the electrophilic atoms C9, C11, C14, N13, N5, N16, and O17. Fukui functions (f-, f+, f0) were employed on SMX to analyze the regioselectivity of the active substance towards SMX to accurately interpret the nucleophilic, electrophilic, and radical reactions occurring at the atoms. According to Table S5, it is evident that in the SMX molecule, N13 is the most susceptible site for radical attack, as indicated by the highest f0 value, followed by N16 and C9. Additionally, N13 exhibits the highest f-value, making it the primary site for electrophilic attack. Since both f0 and f-values of N13 are the highest, it indicates that N13 is the most vulnerable site during degradation (Wang et al., 2022).

To explore the degradation pathway of SMX in the 0.5 PN C photocatalytic system, the reaction solution was analyzed by HPLC-MS and DFT to identify the degradation intermediates of SMX. The details of 19 potential intermediates are listed in Table S6. Based on the structural analysis of the intermediates and related literature, four degradation pathways of SMX in the 0.5 PN C photocatalytic system were ultimately proposed. As illustrated in Fig. 6, path I is an oxidation reaction. Due to N13 being the most active electrophilic potential site in SMX, in the presence of ·OH and h+, the typical oxidation by-product of SMX, P1, is generated. Subsequently, P1 undergoes further hydroxylation to yield P2 and oxidation to form P3 through cleavage of the C–N and C–C bonds due to the electrophilicity of the C11 and C14 atoms C bonds due to electrophilicity of the C11 and C14 atoms. Path II represents a hydroxylation reaction where SMX can undergo substitution with hydroxyl groups to produce P5 and P7, with P6 being a conversion product of P5 attributed to the electrophilic nature of O17 and N16. The aromatic rings were prone to being attacked by
O 2
· ‐
radicals through nucleophilic effects, resulting in a series of hydroxylation processes. The primary reaction sites for the formation of hydroxylated SMX (P9) are the C4 and C6 atoms on the aromatic ring of SMX. Further transformation to P10 results from the breaking of the N5 – S6 bond leading to the formation of P4. By breaking the C4–N5 and S6–C9 bonds, SMX can be hydrolyzed to generate P8, P19, P11, and P12. Pathway III entails a hydrolysis reaction. Hydrolytic demethylation serves as one of the degradation pathways of SMX. Demethylation of SMX to produce the product P13 is initiated by the breaking of the C12–N13 and C2–C1 bonds. Subsequently, P13 is deaminated to yield P14, and the breaking of the S–N bond leads to the formation of products P15 and P16 during the reaction (Li et al., 2021). Path IV involves a self-coupling reaction. The self-coupling product (P17) generated through the coupling reaction between two SMX molecules is further hydroxylated to produce P18. Finally, all intermediate products undergo further reactions to form smaller molecules or mineralize to CO2 and H2O (Wang et al., 2022).

In addiction, the developmental toxicity of SMX and corresponding intermediates was analyzed by using the Toxicity assessment software tool (T.E.S.T). As shown in Fig. S10, with the continuous degradation process of SMX intermediates, their developmental toxicity showed a downward trend, while P4 and P5 were not detected, and it is worth noting that P8, P16 and P19 were identified as developmental nontoxic substances. These findings confirm the detoxification effect of PNC system in the reaction process, and also indicate its potential to achieve mineralization.

In conducting total organic carbon (TOC) experiments, further investigation was carried out to explore the mineralization capability of 0.5 PN C towards SMX under visible irradiation. As shown in Fig. S11, the TOC mineralization efficiency of SMX by the 0.5 PN C system was 76.65%, indicating that this photocatalyst possesses remarkable potential as a water treatment catalyst.

It is well known that pH is one of the important parameters in the aqueous environment. The effect of different pH values on the photocatalytic degradation of SMX was investigated, as depicted in Fig. 7 a and Fig. S12a. The observed rate constants for the SMX degradation by 0.5 PN C were 0.4987, 0.4497, 0.4395, 0.2925, 0.3042, and 0.0440 min−1 at pH values of 3, 5, 5.18 (control blank), 7, 9, and 11, respectively. Excellent SMX degradation efficiency was observed with the 0.5 PN C system in the pH range of 3–9, with all SMX degradation rates above 95% in 15 min. However, at pH values exceeding 11, SMX is predominantly present in anionic state. Under such circumstances, excess OH− ions may vie with SMX for the active agent positions on the photocatalyst surface. This competition contributes to significant degradation in photocatalytic efficacy of 0.5 PN C (Zhou et al., 2024). Consequently, poor photocatalytic degradation of SMX was observed with the 0.5 PN C photocatalytic system under high alkaline conditions.

Research delved into the impact of common ions and soluble organic compounds within the aqueous medium on the process of photocatalytic decomposition of SMX. As depicted in Fig. 7b and Fig. S12b, the degradation of SMX was minimally affected by the presence of
SO 4
2 ‐
, Fe3+, and Mg2+. However, the photocatalytic degradation rate of SMX by 0.5 PN C was inhibited in the presence of Cl−, NO3 −, HCO3 −, Cu2+, and HA. The decrease in the photocatalytic degradation rate can be attributed to several factors: (i) in the presence of Cl−, ·OH, and h+ were consumed, generating Cl− radicals with lower oxidation potentials, thereby causing a reduction in the effectiveness of the photocatalytic process; (ii) the addition of NO3 − ions led to a reduced photocatalytic degradation efficiency possibly due to the reaction of NO3 − with reactive radicals and h+ to produce species such as NO3· and NO2· with oxidation potentials lower than ·OH; (iii) HCO3 − scavenges some of the ·OH in the system, resulting in decreased photocatalytic degradation efficiency (Yang et al., 2022); (vi) when Cu2+ is present, it traps e− and h+ and inhibits the exciton separation ability to some extent (Yang et al., 2020); (v) the decrease in the photocatalytic activity of 0.5 PN C with the addition of HA may be attributed to the light shielding effect and the competitive response of ROS (Zhu et al., 2022). In spite of the existence of ions and soluble organic compounds in the aqueous milieu that influence the 0.5 PN C system, the rates of degradation of SMX were upheld at a level higher than 90%.

In addition, the feasibility of the 0.5 PN C photocatalytic system in different aqueous matrices was further investigated, as shown in Fig. 7c. The efficiency of 0.5 PN C in degrading SMX in the other five aqueous matrices was found to be reduced compared to ultrapure water. These phenomena could be attributed to the scavenging effect of coexisting components in these aqueous matrices on the active species. Nevertheless, a relatively high efficiency in removing SMX was still maintained by the 0.5 PN C photocatalytic system in tap water, lake water, Pearl River water, and WWTP effluent water, with removal rates of 98.61%, 95.71%, 93.33%, and 82.96%, respectively. These demonstrated the splendid applicability of the catalytic system for the remediation of contaminated drinking and environmental water.

Only with high stability, the photocatalyst can maintain its activity during long-term use and achieve an efficient and sustainable photocatalytic degradation process. Hence, the stability of the 0.5 PN C photocatalyst underwent assessment through successive photocatalytic degradation experiments of SMX, conducted under consistent conditions. Illustrated in Fig. 7d, after three cycles of experiments, 0.5 PN C can still maintain a degradation rate of SMX above 80%. SEM observation (Fig. S13a) showed that the morphology of 0.5 PN C after three photocatalytic reactions did not change significantly. Notably, the degradation efficiency of SMX can reach more than 95% again by using ascorbic acid (Fig. S13b). In addition, the 0.5 PN C system has different degradation effects on different drugs (Fig. S14). These results demonstrated that 0.5 PN C has remarkable stability, reusable performance, and the potential to be a versatile degradation material. After ICP-MS analysis, the concentration of copper in and out was approximately 0.91 mg/L, which was lower than the detection requirements of cobalt in China's surface water quality standards (CHN-GB 3838-2002, Cu ≤ 1 mg/L).

In conclusion, a novel photocatalyst, 0.5 PN C, synthesized via a simple ultrasonic stirring method using N-CDs modified PhC2Cu, was successfully developed. Under visible light irradiation, 0.5 PN C demonstrates excellent photocatalytic performance, achieving 99.87% removal of 5 mg L−1 SMX within merely 15 min. This remarkable performance is attributed to the synergistic effect between N-CDs and PhC2Cu, which enhances electron transfer and separation efficiency, consequently boosting pollutant removal by generating more active species. Additionally, various characterizations such as ESR experiments and quenching experiments confirmed the active roles of 1O2,
O 2
· ‐
, and h+ during the photocatalytic process. Finally, DFT calculations and LC-MS analysis were utilized to envisage the plausible degradation intermediates and pathways of SMX. Overall, these findings underscore the enhanced interfacial electron transfer and photoelectrochemical properties of the 0.5 PN C system through N-CDs modification, positioning 0.5 PN C as a promising photocatalyst with significant environmental remediation potential.
